skill.name: explore-code
skill.description: Plan bounded exploratory code changes for deep learning research repositories. Use it to identify candidate edit targets, outline reversible adaptations, and record candidate-level outputs without modifying trusted baselines by default.
skill.summary: Plan isolated, auditable research code adaptations with rollback-aware records.
skill.author: lllllllama

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found the command execution, weak cryptography, sensitive key, traversal, and obfuscation alerts are false positives from prose, Markdown formatting, f-strings, and normal Python syntax. The confirmed risks are bounded filesystem inspection and dynamic loading of a shared parent-directory Python helper. Publish with a warning to run it only in trusted worktrees and verify the shared helper path.

medium_findings.title: Dynamic Parent-Directory Module Loading
medium_findings.description: The output wrapper resolves a shared Python module from a parent directory and executes it with exec_module. This appears to be an intentional shared helper pattern, but it can execute unexpected code if that parent shared path is writable or replaced.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly loads and executes a Python module from a computed filesystem path. The intent is compatibility with shared tooling, so this is a publish warning rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Broad Repository Filesystem Inspection
low_findings.description: The planning script reads caller-provided JSON files and recursively scans the target repository for code and configuration files. This is expected for a code planning skill, but users should run it only on intended repositories and avoid sensitive worktrees.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem behavior is explicit and semantically relevant to the skill purpose. No evidence found of network exfiltration or secret harvesting, which keeps confidence below high severity.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command-Execution Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static command execution alerts in the documentation are Markdown inline code references and output filenames. They do not invoke a shell, subprocess, or external command.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are prose or Markdown code formatting. No executable shell construct is present at these locations.

low_findings.title: Python String Formatting Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static shell backtick alerts in the planning script are f-strings that place values in Markdown-style backticks for generated reports. They do not execute shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines construct strings for output data and printed guidance. There is no subprocess, eval, os.system, or Ruby-style backtick execution.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto And Certificate Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and certificate/key detections are word-pattern matches in descriptions, argparse text, and dictionary key handling. No cryptographic algorithm, certificate file, or private key material was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited contexts are ordinary text or dictionary methods, not crypto APIs or sensitive files. No evidence found of encryption, hashing, certificates, or embedded secrets.

low_findings.title: Traversal And Obfuscation Alerts Are Benign Context
low_findings.description: Relative paths in the skill instructions reference shared documentation, and the obfuscation heuristic points at normal Python syntax. The documentation path escapes the skill directory, but it is a reference to shared repository guidance rather than a file operation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The SKILL.md paths are documentation references, and the Python file is readable source with no packed or encoded payload. The remaining concern is only that marketplace users should know the skill expects shared repository files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dynamic Parent-Directory Module Loading
dangerous_patterns.description: The output wrapper resolves a shared Python module from a parent directory and executes it with exec_module. This appears to be an intentional shared helper pattern, but it can execute unexpected code if that parent shared path is writable or replaced.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code directly loads and executes a Python module from a computed filesystem path. The intent is compatibility with shared tooling, so this is a publish warning rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Repository Filesystem Inspection
dangerous_patterns.description: The planning script reads caller-provided JSON files and recursively scans the target repository for code and configuration files. This is expected for a code planning skill, but users should run it only on intended repositories and avoid sensitive worktrees.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem behavior is explicit and semantically relevant to the skill purpose. No evidence found of network exfiltration or secret harvesting, which keeps confidence below high severity.

content.value_statement: Research code exploration can blur candidate work and trusted baselines. This skill guides isolated, reversible code planning with clear records for later review.
actual_capabilities: Identifies candidate code and configuration files in a target repository.
Builds a minimal patch plan for exploratory model or adapter changes.
Records rollback notes for candidate code modifications.
Creates validation plan items for syntax, imports, configs, and model surfaces.
Separates exploratory candidates from trusted baseline work.
Writes expected exploration bundle outputs through helper scripts.
limitations: It does not prove that a research result is valid or reproducible.
It depends on accurate user context and repository structure.
It should not be used on sensitive repositories without review.
It expects shared repository guidance and helper scripts to be present.

use_cases.title: Plan a Candidate Adapter Change
use_cases.description: Map a LoRA or adapter idea onto likely model files and reversible config changes.

use_cases.title: Review an Exploration Branch
use_cases.description: Create clear records that explain candidate edits, rollback paths, and validation steps.

use_cases.title: Prepare Safe Code Migration
use_cases.description: Identify low-risk transplant points before adapting modules from another research repository.

prompt_templates.title: Find Candidate Targets
prompt_templates.prompt: Use explore-code on this isolated branch. Identify candidate files for a small adapter change and explain why each target matters.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Reversible Patch
prompt_templates.prompt: Use explore-code to create a minimal patch plan for replacing the model head. Include rollback notes and validation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Transplant a Source Module
prompt_templates.prompt: Use explore-code to adapt this source module into the current research repository. Keep changes isolated and record candidate status.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an Exploration Bundle
prompt_templates.prompt: Use explore-code to review the proposed exploration bundle. Check comparability, rollback clarity, protected files, and validation coverage.

output_examples.input: Plan an isolated adapter experiment for a vision model repository.
output: A ranked list of candidate model and configuration files.
A short reversible patch plan with rollback notes.
A validation checklist for syntax, imports, configs, and constructor surfaces.

output_examples.input: Prepare records for an exploratory branch before review.
output: A changeset summary that separates candidate edits from verified claims.
A comparability note that preserves the trusted baseline.
A status summary for the exploration output bundle.

best_practices: Run the skill only on an isolated branch or worktree.
Provide the current research context and experiment branch explicitly.
Review generated plans before applying any code modification.
anti_patterns: Do not use it for trusted baseline reproduction or final claims.
Do not run it on repositories that contain unrelated secrets or private data.
Do not treat candidate outputs as validated experiment results.

faq.question: Does this skill run training jobs?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on planning and recording exploratory code changes. Separate tools should handle training execution.

faq.question: Can it modify the trusted baseline?
faq.answer: It is designed to keep exploratory work isolated from the trusted baseline unless the user explicitly authorizes otherwise.

faq.question: What repositories fit this skill?
faq.answer: It fits deep learning research repositories where candidate model, adapter, head, or config changes need careful planning.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill includes scripts that inspect repository files and one wrapper that loads a shared Python helper from a parent path.

faq.question: Does it send data to a network service?
faq.answer: No evidence found of network requests in the reviewed files.

faq.question: What should users verify before running it?
faq.answer: Users should verify the target worktree, input JSON files, and shared helper path before executing scripts.
